output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.2": 0.5, "R1.3": 0.7, "R1.6": 0.9}}

Revised the section to fix pervasive tense inconsistency, untangle run-on sentences, and flag two evidence-claim gaps that need attention before the prose can be tightened further.

Key issues found
- Tense drifts within nearly every sentence (e.g., "we ran… we recruited… each participant completes… they were asked"). The methods section should be uniformly past tense.
- Long compound sentences chained by "and" obscure the procedural steps; each subsection collapses ~4 actions into one sentence.
- "64 participants" plus "30 trial items" with "counterbalance the mapping between confidence phrases and underlying answer correctness" is under-specified: how many phrases, how the counterbalancing was structured (Latin square? block randomization?), and whether 64 divides cleanly into the design is not stated. Reviewers will ask.
- The mixed-effects model is described as having "confidence phrase as a fixed effect" but the headline result contrasts "numeric phrases" vs. "qualitative phrases" — a two-level contrast, not a per-phrase effect. Either the model included a phrase-type factor, or the contrast was a planned comparison; the current text conflates them.
- "Substantial agreement on the dominant theme" is not an inter-rater statistic. Report κ or percent agreement, and distinguish agreement on coding from agreement that a theme is dominant.
- "Held constant across conditions" needs a number: was accuracy fixed at, e.g., 50%, or matched per phrase?
- "We believe that this finding has implications…" is editorializing inside a results paragraph; move to Discussion or remove the hedge.
- "Furthermore" opening the last paragraph is a discourse marker doing no work; the paragraph is a second qualitative finding plus an interpretive claim.

Revised text

In this section we describe the user study conducted to investigate how participants interpret natural-language confidence expressions produced by a conversational assistant when answering factual questions. We recruited 64 participants through a university subject pool. Each participant completed a 45-minute session in which they read assistant responses paired with verbal confidence phrases (e.g., "high confidence," "fairly sure") and rated how much they trusted each answer on a seven-point scale.

The study interface presented one question at a time, with the assistant's answer and its accompanying confidence phrase shown beneath the question. Participants then advanced to a rating screen, where they entered a numerical trust rating and a short free-text justification; the justifications were later coded for thematic content. The order of the 30 trial items was randomized per participant, and the mapping between confidence phrases and underlying answer correctness was counterbalanced [specify scheme — e.g., Latin square across N blocks] so that no phrase was systematically associated with correct or incorrect answers across the cohort.

We analyzed trust ratings using a mixed-effects model with participant as a random intercept and phrase type (numeric vs. qualitative) as a fixed effect [confirm: was individual phrase nested within type?]. Holding assistant accuracy constant at [X%] across conditions, participants assigned systematically higher trust ratings to answers paired with numeric phrases than to those paired with qualitative phrases (β = [ ], SE = [ ], p = [ ]). Two researchers independently coded the free-text justifications; inter-rater agreement was [κ = / % agreement = ]. The dominant theme was that numeric phrases were perceived as more authoritative.

The qualitative coding further suggested that participants sometimes treated verbal hedges as politeness markers rather than as signals of epistemic uncertainty. [Move interpretive implications to Discussion.]

Uncertainty disclosure
- The counterbalancing scheme, exact accuracy level held constant, and inter-rater statistic are placeholders — I inferred from context that these were performed but the source text does not specify them. Confirm with the original protocol before filling in.
- I assumed "numeric vs. qualitative" was a pre-registered factor in the model; if the analysis instead used per-phrase fixed effects and the type-level contrast was post hoc, that needs disclosure.
